name: tdd-workflows-tdd-red
description: Generate failing tests for the TDD red phase to define expected behavior and edge cases.
capabilities: Generate failing tests following Arrange-Act-Assert pattern for multiple frameworks
Cover edge cases including null values, boundaries, and error conditions
Create framework-specific test structures for Jest, pytest, Go testing, and RSpec
Design property-based tests using fast-check or Hypothesis libraries
Document test execution commands and failure verification steps
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns are code examples in markdown documentation, not executable code. Lines 73-75 show JavaScript mock function syntax (jest.fn), lines 84-85 show Go t.Parallel() method calls, line 88 shows Ruby RSpec let blocks, and lines 134+ contain TypeScript test examples in markdown code blocks. No actual shell execution, cryptographic operations, or system reconnaissance code exists in this skill.
valueStatement: Starting TDD without clear test definitions leads to incomplete coverage. This skill creates comprehensive failing tests that define expected behavior before implementation begins.
userTitle: Generate failing tests for TDD red phase
limitations: Tests require manual verification to ensure they fail for correct reasons
Does not implement the actual code being tested (green phase)
Requires specification of the behavior or feature to test
Framework selection must be specified by the user

useCases.targetUser: Backend developer starting a new user authentication module
useCases.title: New feature TDD workflow
useCases.description: Generate comprehensive failing tests before implementing a new authentication service, covering valid credentials, invalid inputs, and edge cases.

useCases.targetUser: API developer establishing clear interface contracts
useCases.title: API contract testing
useCases.description: Create contract tests that define expected API request and response formats before building the endpoint implementation.

useCases.targetUser: Developer improving test coverage on inherited codebase
useCases.title: Legacy code test coverage
useCases.description: Add missing test coverage to existing code by generating tests that document current behavior before refactoring.

promptTemplates.title: Basic unit test generation
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate failing unit tests for a function that validates email addresses. Use pytest framework. Cover valid emails, invalid formats, empty strings, and null inputs.
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting TDD for a new email validation utility

promptTemplates.title: Service layer testing
promptTemplates.prompt: Create failing tests for a UserService with methods: createUser, getUserById, deleteUser. Use Jest with mocked UserRepository. Include error handling for duplicate emails and missing users.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building user management service with dependency injection

promptTemplates.title: Property-based testing
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate property-based tests for a sorting function using fast-check. Verify that output is always sorted, maintains original length, and preserves all input elements.
promptTemplates.scenario: Testing mathematical invariants for algorithm correctness

promptTemplates.title: Integration test suite
promptTemplates.prompt: Create failing integration tests for a payment processing workflow including: successful payment, declined card, insufficient funds, and network timeout scenarios. Use RSpec with shared examples.
promptTemplates.scenario: End-to-end payment flow testing before implementation

bestPractices: Name tests using should_X_when_Y convention to document expected behavior clearly
Keep tests isolated with no dependencies between test cases
Verify tests fail for the right reason (missing behavior) not setup errors
antiPatterns: Testing implementation details instead of observable behavior
Creating tests that pass immediately without implementing functionality
Using complex setup code that obscures test intent

faq.question: What is the TDD red phase?
faq.answer: The red phase is the first step in Test-Driven Development where you write failing tests that define the expected behavior before writing any implementation code.

faq.question: How do I verify my tests fail for the right reason?
faq.answer: Run each test individually and check that failure messages indicate missing functionality or assertion failures, not syntax errors, import issues, or setup problems.

faq.question: Which testing frameworks are supported?
faq.answer: This skill supports Jest and Vitest for JavaScript/TypeScript, pytest for Python, built-in testing package for Go, and RSpec for Ruby.

faq.question: Should I mock external dependencies?
faq.answer: Yes, mock all external dependencies including databases, APIs, and file systems to ensure tests are fast, reliable, and truly isolated unit tests.

faq.question: How many edge cases should I include?
faq.answer: Cover null/empty inputs, boundary values, special characters, error conditions, and any domain-specific edge cases relevant to your use case.

faq.question: What do I do after generating failing tests?
faq.answer: Move to the green phase by implementing the minimum code required to make all tests pass, then refactor while keeping tests green.
